title: RFC & ADR Processes
description: RFCs (Request for Comments) and ADRs (Architecture Decision Records) provide structured, asynchronous decision-making processes that scale across engineering organizations.

sections.title: RFC Lifecycle
sections.description: The RFC lifecycle has five phases: Proposal (author drafts with problem statement, options, recommendation), Review (team comments in a PR with minimum comment period), Decision (allocated decision-maker approves, rejects, or requests changes), Implementation (tracking issue linked to approved RFC), and Retrospective (post-implementation review). Use RFC PR labels like rfc/needs-review, rfc/approved, rfc/implemented.

sections.title: ADR Templates
sections.description:
Architecture Decision Records capture discrete decisions in a lightweight format. Each ADR includes: title, status (proposed/accepted/deprecated/superseded), context (why this decision was needed), decision (the chosen approach), consequences (trade-offs and impact), and compliance notes. Store ADRs in docs/adr/ with sequential numbering. Reference ADRs in RFCs and PR descriptions.

# ADR-001: Use GitHub Actions for CI

Status: Accepted

Context: Need a CI system that integrates
with GitHub repos natively...

Decision: Use GitHub Actions over Jenkins...

Consequences: +50% faster onboarding...

sections.title: Review Process
sections.description:
RFC reviews follow asynchronous, inclusive deliberation. Set a minimum review period (e.g., 2 business days) and request specific domain experts. Use PR review threads for structured feedback — separate blocking concerns from nice-to-haves. The decision-maker is responsible for final closure. Publish a weekly RFC digest to keep the org informed of pending and recent decisions.

# RFC review checklist
- Problem statement clear?
- Options analyzed with trade-offs?
- Security/compliance reviewed?
- Migration plan included?
- Success criteria defined?

sections.title: Organizational Scaling
sections.description: As orgs grow past 50 engineers, formal RFC/ADR processes prevent decision bottlenecks. Designate area experts (platform, security, data) as required reviewers for relevant RFCs. Create an RFC steering committee for cross-cutting decisions. At 200+ engineers, template RFCs for common patterns (new service, dependency upgrade, API change) to reduce friction. Track adoption via RFC-to-implementation ratio.

sections.title: Tooling & Automation
sections.description:
Automate RFC management with GitHub Actions: validate frontmatter on PR create, check for required reviewers, post comment with review timeline, merge when approved. Use issue forms for RFC submission. Build a dashboard showing RFC metrics: cycle time, approval rate, implementation lag. ADR linting ensures consistency across the repository.

name: rfc-lint
on: pull_request_target
jobs:
  lint:
    if: contains(github.event.pull_request.labels.*.name, 'rfc')
    steps:
      - run: validate-rfc ${{ github.event.pull_request.body }}

details: RFCs and ADRs serve complementary roles: RFCs explore open-ended problems with multiple options, while ADRs record discrete decisions. The RFC process ensures that significant technical decisions are documented, reviewed by domain experts, and approved with explicit buy-in. At companies like Amazon (6-pager), Google (design docs), and GitHub (GH-API RFCs), the RFC process is core to engineering culture. ADRs originated from Michael Nygard's blog post and solve the problem of "why did we do it this way?" — they create an auditable trail of architectural decisions. Each ADR links to related ADRs and the RFC that triggered it. Status tracking is critical: an ADR can be Proposed, Accepted, Deprecated (no longer relevant), or Superseded (a newer ADR replaces it). The Markovian nature of ADRs — each decision building on previous ones — creates an architectural lineage. For tooling, the adr-tools CLI can scaffold new ADRs, and adr-log generates a decision log. In large orgs, the RFC/ADR process should be lightweight enough to not discourage documentation but rigorous enough to maintain quality. The key metric is cycle time: from RFC submission to decision. If cycle time exceeds 2 weeks, the process needs streamlining.
cheatSheet:
Core Frameworks

  • RFC lifecycle: Proposal → Review (PR with comment period) → Decision → Implementation → Retrospective.
  • ADR template: Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Compliance. Stored as docs/adr/NNN-title.md.
  • ADR statuses: Proposed → Accepted → Deprecated / Superseded.

Staff+ Patterns

  • RFCs explore open-ended problems with options; ADRs record discrete decisions. Both create an auditable decision trail.
  • At 50+ engineers, designate area experts as required reviewers for relevant RFCs.
  • Automate RFC validation: frontmatter checks, required reviewer labels, weekly digest of pending RFCs.

Watch Outs

  • RFCs without deadlines languish. Set a mandatory comment period (2 business days) and decision deadline.
  • ADRs that aren't referenced in subsequent decisions become noise. Link ADRs in PR descriptions and related RFCs.
  • Over-formalizing small decisions kills adoption — use ADRs for significant, lasting architecture decisions only.

Interview Angle

Stripe/Amazon L6: "How do you scale technical decision-making from a 10-person startup to 500 engineers?" Answer: Introduce RFCs at ~20 engineers (prevent knowledge silos), ADRs at ~50 engineers (answer "why did we do it this way?"), and RFC steering committees at ~200 engineers for cross-cutting decisions.
